title: TabPFN-2.5: Advancing the State of the Art in Tabular Foundation Models
authors: Prior Labs Team (Noah Hollmann, Samuel Müller, Lennart Purucker, Frank Hutter, et al.; contributor list in Appendix A)
venue: arXiv:2511.08667 (technical report, Prior Labs, November 2025). NOTE: the dispatch URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.16078 was WRONG — 2509.16078 is an unrelated paper (MTS-DMAE, ICDM 2025). The correct TabPFN-2.5 paper is arXiv:2511.08667.
tokenization_method: Same per-CELL tokenization + two-way (feature-axis AttnFeat, sample-axis AttnSamp) dual attention as TabPFN v2, scaled up. One token per (sample, feature) cell; a FeatureEncoder embeds each cell value, a TargetEncoder embeds labels, alternating feature/sample attention runs over a deeper stack. The v2 core — 'feature/sample dual attention, caching separation of training/test context, and positional feature embeddings — remain unchanged.' What CHANGED in 2.5: (1) depth 12 -> 18 layers (regression) / 12 -> 24 layers (classification); (2) the per-attention-block FEATURE GROUP SIZE increased from 2 to 3 cells per group (compresses the feature-axis sequence ~1.5x for faster train/inference and more features per memory budget); (3) regression FeatureEncoder upgraded from linear to a 2-layer MLP; (4) 64 learned 'thinking' rows appended to the in-context set (LLM pause-token analogue) providing extra compute and acting as attention sinks; (5) inference-time test-time augmentation: predictions aggregated across multiple dataset permutations + feature transformations (robust scaling, soft clipping, quantile + standard scaling, and appended SVD components as extra features).
feature_types_handled: numerical, categorical, missing, outliers — mixed, natively, no manual scaling/one-hot/imputation (carried over from v2). No native temporal/time-varying axis: each row is a static feature vector. A separate model TabPFN-TS handles time series by injecting temporal context into the in-context set; TabPFN-2.5 itself does not.
token_granularity: per-cell, but with feature GROUPING: groups of 3 adjacent feature-cells are bundled per attention block (v2 used groups of 2). So the effective unit along the feature axis is a 3-cell group rather than a single cell — a small but load-bearing change that buys the 4x feature-count headroom (500 -> 2,000) at fixed attention cost.
numerical_strategy: raw values after internal normalization; richness comes from the synthetic prior + dual attention, not a hand-designed numerical embedding (same philosophy as v2). NEW: regression uses a 2-layer-MLP FeatureEncoder instead of linear; inference applies robust scaling, soft clipping, quantile + standard-scaling transforms and aggregates over them (test-time augmentation), plus appends SVD components as synthetic extra features.
categorical_strategy: passed directly, embedded by the shared FeatureEncoder into the same cell-token space (no per-feature lookup table); category structure learned from the synthetic prior. No change from v2 in mechanism.
high_cardinality_handling: no dedicated high-cardinality mechanism documented (same gap as v2). Because there is no per-feature category lookup table, parameter count does not blow up with cardinality, but the paper gives no extreme-cardinality or text-field treatment. High-cardinality remains an unaddressed regime.
temporal_handling: none in TabPFN-2.5. Time-varying features must be hand-flattened into static columns. (Separate TabPFN-TS extension exists for forecasting via temporal context in the in-context set — out of scope for this model.)
scales_to_many_features: The headline scaling claim: validated for up to 50,000 samples x 2,000 features — a 5x row and 4x feature increase over v2, ~20x more data cells. Exploratory (unvalidated) runs reached ~160k rows x 500 features (classification) / ~85k x 500 (regression) on an H100 80GB in FP16. The feature-grouping (size 3) + deeper stack are the mechanism enabling the 4x feature jump. STILL a small/medium-table model: 2,000 features is the validated ceiling; >=70 features is comfortably inside range, but the paper explicitly names 'scaling to millions of rows' as the unmet next step. Inference: ~1s at 10k rows, ~10s at 50k rows (H100, 500 test rows).
downstream_model: the Transformer IS the model (frozen pretrained PFN, in-context prediction in one forward pass, no per-dataset training). NEW deployment path: a proprietary 'distillation engine' converts a fitted TabPFN-2.5 into a dataset-specific small MLP or tree ensemble that does single-point (non-in-context) inference at orders-of-magnitude lower latency while preserving most of the accuracy.
pretraining_objective: in-context Bayesian-style prediction on PURELY synthetic data (no real data in base pretraining). The synthetic prior was substantially improved: broadened distribution set, scaled to more rows/features, while keeping prediction tasks hard. Real-TabPFN-2.5 is a fine-tuned variant: base model fine-tuned on 43 real-world tabular datasets (OpenML + Kaggle), deduplicated against all internal benchmarks — and it outperforms the synthetic-only base.
key_results: TabArena (living benchmark): TabPFN-2.5 is the best DEFAULT model, substantially beating tuned tree-based models and matching/approaching AutoGluon 1.4 'extreme' (a 4-hour tuned ensemble that itself includes TabPFN v2). On TabArena-Lite classification it outperforms AutoGluon 1.4 in a single forward pass; Real-TabPFN-2.5 widens the lead. Win rates vs default XGBoost: 100% on small/medium classification (<=10k samples, 500 features); 87% on larger sets up to 100k samples x 2k features (85% for regression). vs TabPFN v2: 'often performs much better and is never much worse.' Regression benefits more from tuning (60 configs vs 200 for baselines to match AutoGluon).
baselines_compared: Default & tuned XGBoost, tuned tree-based models (GBDTs), AutoGluon 1.4 'extreme' (4-hour tuned ensemble), TabPFN v2, and (briefly, Appendix G) concurrent tabular foundation models TabICL and LimiX. Primary emphasis on AutoGluon and XGBoost.
transferable_idea: Feature GROUPING inside the per-cell two-way-attention stack: bundling adjacent feature-cells into groups of 3 (vs v2's 2) compresses the feature-axis sequence and is the lever that took the validated feature ceiling from ~500 to 2,000 at roughly fixed attention cost. For the 70+-heterogeneous-feature target, the directly reusable ideas are: (a) feature-axis attention with cell GROUPING as a cheap way to extend a permutation-invariant feature tokenizer to many more columns; (b) 'thinking rows' as learned extra-compute / attention-sink slots; (c) test-time augmentation over feature transformations + appended SVD pseudo-features as a free accuracy lever for any tabular tokenizer; (d) the distillation-to-MLP/tree path as a way to deploy an expensive in-context tokenizer at low latency.
limitations: Section 7 is thin on explicit limitations. Stated/observed: (1) validated only up to 50,000 rows x 2,000 features; 'scaling to millions of rows' is named as the unmet next step. (2) Larger row/feature runs are exploratory and 'outside our validated range.' (3) No native temporal support. (4) No high-cardinality / text-feature treatment. (5) Regression needs more tuning than classification to reach top accuracy. (6) The win-rate-vs-XGBoost framing uses DEFAULT XGBoost for the 100% small-data claim — a weaker baseline than the tuned-GBDT comparison. (7) Core distillation engine is PROPRIETARY (not reproducible) and weights are under a non-commercial license.
pitfalls: 1) The arXiv URL in the dispatch (2509.16078) is WRONG and points to an unrelated paper; correct ID is 2511.08667 — anyone citing the dispatch URL would cite the wrong work. 2) 'Advancing the state of the art' is a Prior Labs technical report (vendor self-report), not peer-reviewed; the 100% win rate is vs DEFAULT (untuned) XGBoost — the genuinely strong claim is the 87% rate and the AutoGluon-1.4-matching result. 3) The distillation engine that enables low-latency deployment is PROPRIETARY and undisclosed — the most production-relevant contribution is not reproducible from the paper. 4) 2,000 features is the VALIDATED ceiling but is not the same as good behavior at high feature counts with few rows — concurrent work (TabPFN-Wide, arXiv:2510.06162) exists precisely because extreme feature counts remain a separate problem. 5) Real-TabPFN-2.5's gains come from fine-tuning on 43 real datasets — benchmark-leakage risk is mitigated by dedup but the gap between synthetic-only and real-tuned is a reminder the synthetic prior is still imperfect. 6) Non-commercial license blocks production use without a paid enterprise license.
relevance_to_topic: The current SOTA tabular foundation model and direct successor to TabPFN v2: it pushes the validated feature ceiling to 2,000 (4x) and rows to 50k (5x) via feature-cell GROUPING + a deeper dual-attention stack, comfortably covering the topic's 70+-heterogeneous-feature target on small/medium tables — and its grouping trick, thinking rows, TTA-over-transforms, and distillation-to-MLP path are concrete, transferable tokenizer-scaling levers (though temporal, high-cardinality, and many-rows regimes remain open).
code_available: yes (partial) — pip install tabpfn (local) or tabpfn-client (cloud); weights on Hugging Face (Prior-Labs/tabpfn_2_5). License: TABPFN-2.5 License v1.0 — permissive for research/internal eval, PROHIBITS commercial/production use and competitive benchmarking; commercial enterprise license available separately. The distillation engine is proprietary and not released.
